events.name: Past Time of Magic
events.description: If you’ll believe me there was a time when the fairies were none so shy as they are now. That was the time when beasts talked to men, when there were spells and enchantments and magic every day, when there was great store of hidden treasure to be dug up, and adventures for the asking.

events.name: Introducing the couple.
events.description: At that time, you must know, an old man and an old woman lived alone by themselves. They were good and they were poor and they had no children at all.

events.name: They Prepare for Day's Tasks.
events.description: One fine day, “What are you doing this morning, good man?” says the old woman. “Oh,” says the old man, “I’m off to the mountains with my billhook to gather a faggot of sticks for our fire. And what are you doing, good wife?” “Oh,” says the old woman, “I’m off to the stream to wash clothes. It’s my washing day,” she adds.
thoughts: The provided text appears to be a mundane conversation between an old man and woman, setting up their daily routine. Given that the previous narrative context establishes an idyllic past where magic was present, this event can be seen as part of the setup for a more significant story to come, but in itself it is simply a depiction of daily life.

events.name: The Old Woman Washes Clothes.
events.description: So the old man went to the mountains and the old woman went to the stream. Now, while she was washing the clothes, what should she see but a fine ripe peach that came floating down the stream? The peach was big enough, and rosy red on both sides.

events.name: She Finds Lucky Peach
events.description: “I’m in luck this morning,” said the dame, and she pulled the peach to shore with a split bamboo stick. By-and-by, when her good man came home from the hills, she set the peach before him. “Eat, good man,” she said; “this is a lucky peach I found in the stream and brought home for you.”
thoughts: The provided text appears to be a continuation of the old woman's day, where she encounters and collects a peach from the stream. This action is part of her daily routine and doesn't directly drive the conflict or main action of the story.

events.name: Peach Turns into Baby Boy
events.description: But the old man never got a taste of the peach. And why did he not? All of a sudden the peach burst in two and there was no stone to it, but a fine boy baby where the stone should have been.
thoughts: The peach bursting into two and revealing a baby instead of a stone is an unexpected twist that adds surprise and wonder to the story. This event can be seen as part of the setup for the main action or conflict in the narrative.

events.name: Boy Born from Peach
events.description: “Momotaro! Momotaro!” cries the old man; “the eldest son of the peach.” “Truth it is indeed,” says the old woman; “he was born in a peach.”

events.name: Momotaro Grows Stronger
events.description: Both of them took such good care of Momotaro that soon he was the stoutest and bravest boy of all that country-side. He was a credit to them, you may believe. The neighbours nodded their heads and they said, “Momotaro is the fine young man!”

events.name: Momotaro Heads to Ogres' Island
events.description: “Where are you going, Momotaro?” says his mother. “I’m off to the Ogres’ Island,” says Momotaro, “to get their treasure, and I should be obliged if you’d let me have the kimi-dango as soon as may be,” he says.

events.name: Momotaro Departs Island
events.description: So they made him the kimi-dango, and he put them in a wallet, and he tied the wallet to his girdle and off he set. “Sayonara, and good luck to you, Momotaro!” cried the old man and the old woman. “Sayonara! Sayonara!” cried Momotaro.

events.name: Momotaro Outlines Battle Plan
events.description: “Now, brothers,” says Momotaro, “listen to my plan. The pheasant must fly over the castle gate and peck the Ogres. The monkey must climb over the castle wall and pinch the Ogres. The dog and I will break the bolts and bars. He will bite the Ogres, and I will fight the Ogres.”

events.description: The brave companions fought till sundown and overcame the Ogres. Those that were left alive they took prisoners and bound with cords—a wicked lot they were.

events.name: Momotaro Defeats the Ogres
events.description: “Kia! Kia!” says the monkey. “Thanks, my Lord Momotaro.” “Ken! Ken!” says the pheasant. “Thanks, my Lord Momotaro.” “Bow! Wow! Wow!” says the dog. “Thanks, my dear Lord Momotaro.”
thoughts: The story has reached its culmination with the successful retrieval of the Ogres' treasure and the defeat of the Ogres. This marks a turning point in the narrative as it transitions from the conflict to the resolution.
